---
title: SASS Basics
instalment: 184
creators: [bart, allison]
date: 2026-05-09
---

In the previous instalment we dipped our toes into costuming Bootstrap using just CSS variables in the browser, but as we discovered at the end of that instalment, that's not the best way to customise Bootstrap, SASS is.  We've also discovered during our exploration of the Jekyll static-site-generator that it has built-in support for SASS, so this is clearly a technology it would be valuable for us to learn.

This instalment is intended to give a basic overview of how SASS works, enough to start making valuable use of it, but it's going to be nowhere near complete! Ideally this instalment will whet your appetite to start using SASS to supercharge your CSS, and teach you enough to get started, and share enough of the concepts and the jargon that you'll be able to learn the more advanced features and usages from the documentation, which is genuinely good, a lot better than I generally expect from open source projects!

In it's written form this instalment needs to be a single unit, but there's too much detail to record it as a single podcast episode, so if you're listening along there will be a part a and a part b, but if you prefer to read, you'll find a single (hopefully) coherent story here.

We're going to start with a whistle-stop tour of all the features with just simple feature-specific examples, and then we're going to combine as much of what we learned as we can into a single, relatively realistic, worked example.

## SASS — Syntactically Awesome Style Sheets

The reason SASS exists is to automate the creation of regular cascading style sheets (CSS) by adding powerful features missing from CSS itself. SASS is a so-called *CSS pre-processor*, and is the defecto winner in the category, having out-lasted the other early contenders in the space. The runner up was LESS (*Leaner Style Sheets*), but even that's almost completed fallen out of use at this stage.

The process is simple:

1. Write your style sheets in SASS
2. Compile your SASS stylesheet to CSS
3. Import the compiled CSS into your HTML files using a `<link rel="stylesheet">` tag in the `<head>`, or an  `@import`  at-rule in a `<style>` tag.

For historical reasons, the SASS pre-processor accepts two different syntaxes — the original indent-based SASS syntax, and the newer curly-braces-based SCSS syntax. The newer SCSS syntax is a super-set of regular CSS, so developers generally find it easier to learn, and because it uses curly braces to group statements rather than relying purely on indentation, it's more robust when copy-and-pasting. **We will only be using the newer SCSS syntax in this series**!

Yes, this is very confusing! We are going to be using a piece of software named SASS that supports two syntaxes, one of which is also named *SASS* and saved in `.sass` files, while to the other is named the *SCSS syntax* and saved in `.scss` files, both of which are referred to as *SASS files*, and both of which can be converted to regular CSS with the `sass` command.

To keep things as un-unclear as possible, we're going to utterly ignore the older SASS syntax when writing our SASS files, and write all our SASS in the newer SCSS syntax 🤯

From this sentence on we've going to completely forget the old obsolete SASS syntax ever existed, we're going to just accept that our SASS files have a `.scss` file extension, and we're going to just refer to our SASS code in SCSS syntax as simply _'SASS code'_!

## The SASS Basics

Again, just a reminder that **we need to save all our SASS code in files with the `.scss` file extension** because we'll be exclusively using the new syntax and completely ignoring the old syntax!

Secondly, because SASS is a super-set of CSS, **all valid CSS code is valid SASS code**!

In addition to being able to do everything CSS can do, SASS can do things CSS just can't.

Here's a very quick overview of the features we'll be exploring:

* **Selector nesting** — using curly braces to control the targeting of style declarations more clearly
* **Style inheritance** — adopting the styles from one CSS selector into another, and then expanding on them
* **SASS Variables** — both much simpler to use and more powerful than CSS custom properties, 
* **Conditionals and Loops** — implemented as SASS-only at-rules, things like `@if`, `@each` etc..
* **SASS Operators, Functions, and Modules** — as we explore variables, conditionals, and loops we'll slowly meet the arithmetic, string, and comparison operates SASS provides, as well as some of the functions provided by some of SASS's built-in modules.
* **Mixins** — SASS's implantation of templates

### The Atoms of a SASS Style Sheet

Because SASS is a super-set of CSS, the pieces used to assemble as SASS stylesheet will look familiar:

1. **CSS Style Declarations** — definitions for *style properties* applied to different parts of an HTML document via *selectors*
2. **CSS Style Properties** — the named pieces of styling information contained within every HTML element that determine how it gets rendered within the page. For example, the element's colour, the type face to be used for any text within the element, how much padding there should be around the element, and so on.
3. **Selectors** — a syntax for expressing the sets of elements style declarations get applied to, e.g. `p` applies declarations to all paragraphs, `#waffle` applies declarations to just the element with the ID `waffles`, and `p.pancakes` applies declarations to all paragraphs with the *class* `pancakes`.
4. **Custom CSS Properties** — also known as *CSS Variables* are associated with HTML elements in the same way regular style properties are, but their values do not directly affect the page's presentation, instead, they can be referenced in regular style declarations to control their effect (see [instalment 183](./pbs183) for the details).
5. **SASS Variables** — traditional variables that exist in SASS but not in CSS, the SASS compiler converts them to regular CSS values at compile-time.
6. **SASS Operators** — a small set of the usual mathematical and boolean operators like `==`, `!=`, `+`, `-` etc..
7. **CSS at-rules** — CSS statements starting with an `@` that to perform special actions (rather than simply defining a style declaration), e.g. `@property` for defining CSS custom properties.
8. **SASS at-rules** — special SASS statements that look like CSS at-rules but don't exist in CSS. They provide SASS-specific functionally and get converted to regular CSS in an appropriate way at compile-time. For example, `@extend`, `@if` & `@each`.
9. **CSS Functions** — named computations and transformations that take arguments and convert them to values that can be used in style declarations. These are evaluated in the browser when the page is rendered.
10. **SASS Functions & Modules**— SASS functions share the same syntax as CSS functions but don't exist in CSS. They get execute at compile-time, and the values they return get rendered into the output CSS. SASS groups the functions it provides into separate namespaces known as *modules*.
11. **Mixins** — re-usable templates that allow you to avoid code duplication and simplify your style sheets.
12. **SASS Modules** — named collections of SASS functions, variables, mixins, etc., that can be loaded into your code with the `@use` SASS at-rule.

### Data Types in SASS

Again, because SASS is a super-set of CSS, all valid CSS values are valid SASS values.

That means that SASS can deal with colours like `#cococo`, dimensions like `45px`, `24%`, `3rem`, bare keywords like `solid`, numbers like `42` & `3.1415`, and strings enclosed in single or double quotes (which ever is most convenient at the time).

Since SASS exists to generate CSS, only values that are valid in CSS can be output by SASS. However, SASS provides additional more advanced data type for internal use within SASS. Most notably, SASS supports both lists and dictionaries.

The official SASS jargon refers to what we call lists as *Lists*, which is very convenient, and it refers to what we call dictionaries as *Maps*. This is actually a nice term for dictionaries because they provide mappings between names and matching values!

### Comments — SASS+CSS or SASS-Only

SASS supports two types of comments, CSS commends, and SASS comments, and while neither get executed by the SADD compiler, it does treat them very differently!

Standard CSS comments (`/* … */`) in SASS files get passed through to the generated CSS, so they remain visible after compilation.

SASS comments (`// …`)  on the other had get swallowed by the SASS compiler, and disappear from the generated CSS.

```scss
/* This is a comment in both SASS and CSS, and it will appear in the generated CSS */
/* Use comments like this to annotate the generated CSS */

// This on the other hand is a SASS comment, it will not appear in the generated CSS
// Use comments like this to describe SASS-specific aspects of your code
```

### Useful Resources

* The official SASS homepage — [sass-lang.com/…](https://sass-lang.com/)
* The official quick-start guide — [sass-lang.com/…](https://sass-lang.com/guide/) (really nice quick overview)
* The documentation — [sass-lang.com/…](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/)

## Installing the SASS Command Line App

Many static site generators like Jekyll include SASS compilation libraries into their own code, allowing them to handle the SASS compilation for you. But to experiment with SASS without leveraging other tools we need to install a SASS command line tool. There are multiple versions of this tool available for all the major OSes, and for common platforms like NodeJS. You can find the full list on [the official install page](https://sass-lang.com/install/).

Because we have already used NodeJS earlier in this series, let's install the NodeJS version as a globally available module:

```sh
sudo npm install -g sass
```

Once you have the NodeJS version of `sass` installed you can compile SCSS files to CSS files with commands of the form:

```sh
npx sass source_file.scss output_file.css
```

> Note that the `npx` prefix is only needed because we are using the NodeJS version of SASS, if you install a native version you can omit the `npx`. Remember, `npx` is NodeJS's mechanism for finding and executing commands provided by installed modules.
{: .aside}

## Selector Nesting

One of SASS's most useful features is the ability to nest style decorations, making it both easier to define styles that should only apply to nested sub-sets of a page, and, makes the code much easier to read, so it's clearer what styles are intended to apply where.

To see this in action, let's target just the header region for some changes by adding the following SASS code to the bottom of `index.scss`:

```scss
/* style the header region */
header {
  /* Style the page-level header without affecting other headers */
  h1 {
    font-family: 'Ubuntu Sans', sans-serif;
    font-weight: 100;
    font-size: 5rem;
  }

  /* Style the instrunctions */
  section {
    /* clearly enclose the instructions */
    border: 1px solid #00408d;
    border-radius: 0.5em;
    padding: 1em;
    background-color: color-mix(in srgb, #00408d, white 90%);

    /* clear space around the instructions */
    margin-bottom: 2rem;

    /* make the text more subtle */
    color: #00408d;
    font-size: 0.8rem;

    /* compress the paragraphs a little and help the lead stand out */
    p {
      margin-bottom: 0.8em;
      .lead{
        font-weight: bold;
      }
    }

    /* help the code snippets stand out */
    code {
      color: DimGray;
    }
    pre {
      border-left: 3px solid DimGray;
      padding-left: 1em;
      margin-bottom: 0.8em;
    }
  }
}
```

Save the file, watch the running `sass` command re-build the file, then refresh the page to se the effect of this code.

Notice that this code looks *almost* like regular CSS, but with one difference — CSS does not allow style definitions to be nested inside each other.

What SASS does is interpret nesting as selector building, so that means this snippet:

```scss
header {
  /* … */
  section {
    /* … */
    p {
      /* … */
      .lead{
        font-weight: bold;
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Gets compiled down to:

```css
header section p .lead {
  font-weight: bold;
}
```

This shows how SASS's nesting lets us more clearly express that we want the style to apply only top elements with the class `.lead` inside a `<p>` tag inside a `<section>` tag inside a `<header>` tag! 

Ultimately, this does not give us any new functionality, but it does allow us to make our code easier to read, understand, debug, and maintain, which is actually extremely valuable!

## Including Existing Style Declarations in New Declarations with `@extend`

It's quite common to already have a style applied to one selector and to want to apply the same or a similar style to another selector, perhaps to add to it, or, to override just one value.

Imagine you had a defined a style for all block quotes with:

```scss
blockquote {
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
  padding: 3rem;
  font-size: 1.25rem;
}
```

To use the same style for paragraphs with the `.quotation` class but to add a border on the left-hand side you can use `@extend` to import all your styles for `blockquote` into your new definition for `p.quotation` with:

```scss
p.quotation {
  @extend blockquote;
  border-left: 5px solid DarkGray;
}
```

If you add both of those definitions to the playground file and let it compile you'll see that SASS transforms this very readable SCSS code into the equivalent, but optimised pair of CSS definitions:

```css
blockquote, p.quotation {
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
  padding: 3rem;
  font-size: 1.25rem;
}

p.quotation {
  border-left: 5px solid DarkGray;
}
```

The parts of the style that are inherited don't get duplicated, instead, SASS adds both selectors to the common part, and only the additional declarations to the separate selector for `p.quotation`.

## SASS Variables

Unlike CSS custom properties, SASS variables really do behave like the variables in typical programming languages. Their scope is even (mostly) defined by curly braces (more on that later).

Variables must be named with a `$` prefix, and they get assigned values using the same syntax as style declarations, so, for example:

```scss
$my-variable: "Hello World!";
```

By convention they are named in all lower-case with and dash-separated, like CSS properties.

Unlike CSS custom properties, you don't need to do anything special to access a variable, just use it's name as a value:

```scss
$heading-color: #00408d;

h1 {
  color: $heading-color;
}
```

### Basic Math & String Operators

When working with strings or numbers it can be useful to perform basic operations like arithmetic and concatenation. SASS does not provide many operators, but it does provide most of the usual suspects:

* String concatenation is with `+`, e.g `$new-string: $one-string + $another-string;`
* Four of the five basic arithmetic operators have operators:
  * `+` & `-` for addition
  * `*` for multiplication
  * `%` for modulus (integer division remainder)

So what's missing? **There is no division operator** (or rather there was, and it is now deprecated so you should pretend there isn't)!

Does that mean you can't divide in SASS? Not really, but it does mean that the syntax for division is awkward. So awkward in fact that it's often much simpler to **avoid division by using the matching multiplication instead**, for example, rather than divide by `2`, simply multiply by `0.5`! Personally, I rarely resort to division because usually I'm just looking for a simple fraction, and multiplication works just as easily!

But you can of course divide when you really need to, how? By using a the Math module!

### Advanced Math (and Division!) with the `sass:math` Module

Like with every SASS module, you need to tell the compiler to load the Math module (`sass:math`) before you can use it. You do this by adding the appropriate `@use` SASS at-rule to the top of your code. To use the Math module that means need to add:

```scss
@use "sass:math";
```

This module provides a lot of variables and functions, and you'll find all the details in [the module's documentation](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/modules/math/), but here are some of the highlights:

* The Math module provides some variables as well as functions, most notably `math.$pi`.
* `math.div($number-one, $number-two)` — the only non-depredated way to perform division in SASS.
* SASS offers the usual exponential functions like `math.sqrt($number)` to get the square root, and `math.pow($number, $power)` to raise a number to any power.
* `math.abs($number)` get's the *absolute value* of any number, i.e. forces it to it's positive version.
* SASS offers a nice suite of rounding functions:
  * There are the usual suspects like `math.floor($number)`, `math.ceil($number)` & `math.round($number)`.
  * There's also the more unusual but quite useful `math.clamp($min, $number, $max)`, which will force a number into a valid range, if it's too small it's forced to the minimum value, and if it's too big, to the maximum.

### Interpolation with `#{}`

As we've seen, to use a variable's value as a value for a style declaration you don't have to do anything more than use it's name.

But you can use variables in many more places — in fact, you can use them just about everywhere ([see the documentation for full details](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/interpolation/)), but not directly, to use the value of a variable as anything other than the value of a style declaration you need to access it using the interpolation operator (`#{}`).

One of the most common places to see interpolation used is in CSS style property names. As an example, let's use variables to add a border to block quotes that will have a default with on three sides, and then a different width on one, variable, highlight side:

```scss
$border-color: #00408d;
$border-style: solid;
$border-width: 1px;
$border-highlight-side: 'left';
$border-highlight-width: 5px;

blockquote {
  border: $border-width $border-style $border-color;
  border-#{$border-highlight-side}: $border-highlight-width $border-style $border-color;
}
```

This compiles to the following CSS:

```css
blockquote {
  border: 1px solid #00408d;
  border-left: 5px solid #00408d;
}
```

Notice that the property name with interpolation `border-#{$border-highlight-side}` became `border-left`, showing the value of a variable being used as part of a CSS property name thanks to the magic of interpolation!

There are two other important things to note about interpolation in SASS:

1. You can **use interpolation inside strings**, this can be particularly useful for debugging.
2. You can use the interpolation syntax to insert **any value** not just the value from a variable, most importantly, it **works with functions**!

### Debugging Variables with the `@debug` SASS At-Rule & `sass:meta` Module

Once you start using variables in your SASS code it's only a matter of time until you find yourself wanting to peer inside a variable to see its value. How else are you going to get to the bottom of that annoying bug that's driving you nuts!

SASS offers are few useful tools for this, the most important of which is the `@debug` SASS at-rule.

You can include lines of the form `@debug any_value;` (e.g. `@debug $my-variable;`) anywhere in your code, and when you compile the SASS a debug message will be written to the standard error stream on your terminal pre-fixed with `DEBUG: ` and annotated with a line number. This tells you both what the value was, and where in your code it had that value.

For basic data types `@debug` may be all you need, but as you start to use more advanced types like lists and maps you're likely to want a little more information about your variables. This is where the suite of optional functions made available through the standard module `sass:meta` comes in. 

The `sass:meta` modules contains a lot of functions, many of which are quite advanced, so we're going to limit ourselves to the ones that are most useful for debugging. But, if you're curious or looking for something specific, [the module's  documentation](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/modules/meta/) has all the details.

Like with every SASS module, you need to tell the compiler to load if for you by adding an appropriate `@use` SASS at-rule to the top of your code, for the `sass:meta` module you need to add:

```scss
@use "sass:meta";
```

These are the most useful functions for debugging:

* `meta.inspect($value)` — takes any value of any type and prints it, intended for debugging complex data types.
* `meta.type-of($value)` — reveals the data type for a given value, also intended for debugging.
* `meta.variable-exists("name")`  & `meta.global-variable-exists("name")`— return booleans to indicate whether or not a variable or global variable has been defined — very useful for conditionals (`@if`). **Note:** the name of the variable should be passed as a string!

### Lists

SASS lists are just lists of multiple values, like arrays in traditional programming languages. While the concept is similar, the syntax is unusually flexible, depending on what looks the clearest, and whether or not you want nesting, you can use any of the following syntaxes:

```scss
$my-space-delimited-bare-list: item1 item2 item3;
$my-comma-delimited-bare-list: item1, item2, item3;
$my-space-delimited-braced-list: (item1 item2 item3);
$my-space-delimited-squre-bracketed-list: [item1 item2 item3];
$my-nested-list: ([list1-item2 list1-item2], [list2-item1 list2-item2]); // like a 2D array
```

Lists can be used as values for CSS properties that accept multiple values, like font-family.

An interesting quirk is that **lists are one-indexed** rather than zero-indexed, so the first item in a list has the index `1`.

SASS provides a number of useful list-related functions via the `sass:lists` module. As with all modules it has to be loaded at the start of your with with:

```scss
@use 'sass:list';
```

The full list of functions is described in [the module's documentation](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/modules/list/), but let's use an example to explore the most useful ones. Save the following to a file named `temp.scss` :

```scss
@use 'sass:list';
@use 'sass:meta';

// define a sample list
$deserts: waffles, pancakes, ice cream;

// get the number of elements
@debug "There are #{list.length($deserts)} deserts!";

// access specific elements in the list:
@debug "First desert: #{list.nth($deserts, 1)}";
@debug "Second desert: #{list.nth($deserts, 2)}";
@debug "Last desert: #{list.nth($deserts, -1)}";

// add a desert to the list
$deserts: list.append($deserts, cake); // note the assignment back to $deserts!
@debug "Updated list: #{$deserts}";

// find the index for cake
@debug "Cake is at index #{list.index($deserts, cake)}";

// join arrays
$british-deserts: scone, crumpet;
$deserts: list.join($deserts, $british-deserts); // note the assignment back to $deserts!

// inspect an array
@debug "The final list: #{meta.inspect($deserts)}";
```

Since we don't actually need the generated CSS, we can use a little terminal trickery to compile the file, generate the debug messages, and then redirect the output to nothingness:

```sh
npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss > /dev/null
```

This produces the following output:

```text
-:8 DEBUG: There are 3 deserts!
-:11 DEBUG: First desert: waffles
-:12 DEBUG: Second desert: pancakes
-:13 DEBUG: Last desert: ice cream
-:17 DEBUG: Updated list: waffles, pancakes, ice cream, cake
-:20 DEBUG: Cake is at index 4
-:27 DEBUG: The final list: waffles, pancakes, ice cream, cake, scone, crumpet
```

I want to draw your attention to one very important detail **lists are immutable**! That is to say, they **cannot be edited** once they are created. This means that list-altering functions like `list.append()` & `list.join` actually create entirely new list, which is why we need to explicitly save them back to a variable!

### Maps (Dictionaries)

The concept we refer to as *dictionaries* in this series, key-value pairs, are available as *maps* in SASS.

The syntax for SASS maps is a lot less flexible than the syntax for lists, there is just one way to declare a map:

```scss
$my-map: ("key1": value1, "key2": value2);
```

One thing to note about the syntax is that is can be spread over multiple lines, which is very much the convention for even moderately long maps.

Like with lists, SASS provides functions for working with maps via a module, and again, like with lists, we need to load it by adding the following to the start of our script:

```scss
@use "sass:map";
```

Again, like with lists, the module provides more functions that we need, and you can find the details in [the module's documentation](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/modules/map/).

Let's see some of the most useful map functions in action. Save the following to `temp.scss`:

```scss
@use "sass:map";
@use "sass:list";
@use 'sass:meta';

// define a pair of maps that map weight names to weight values
$weight-names: ( // the official names
  "thin": 100,
  "extra-light": 200,
  "light": 300,
  "normal": 400,
  "medium": 500,
  "semi-bold": 600,
  "bold": 700,
  "extra-bold": 800,
  "black": 900
);
$weight-name-aliases: ( // common alternative names for weights
  "ultra-light": 200,
  "regular": 400,
  "demi-bold": 600,
  "ultra-bold": 800,
  "heavy": 900
);

// get the keys from a map
@debug "The canonical weights are: #{meta.inspect(map.keys($weight-names))}";

// get the values from a map
@debug "There are aliases for the following values: #{meta.inspect(map.values($weight-name-aliases))}";

// get the size of a map (number of key-value pairs)
@debug("There are #{list.length(map.keys($weight-names))} canonical weights");

// check if a key exists in the map
@debug("Is 'demi-bold' a canonical weight? #{map.has-key($weight-names, 'demi-bold')}");
@debug("Is there an alias 'demi-bold'? #{map.has-key($weight-name-aliases, 'demi-bold')}");

// access the value for a key
@debug "The typographic term 'semi-bold' maps to font-weight: #{map.get($weight-names, 'semi-bold')}";

// add a key-value pair
$weight-names: map.set($weight-names, 'regular', 400);

// merge two maps — key confilicts OK, value from second map used
$weight-names-all: map.merge($weight-names, $weight-name-aliases);

// inspect a map
@debug "All weights & aliases: #{meta.inspect($weight-names-all)}";
```

Running this with the same command we used for lists (`npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss > /dev/null`) produces the following output:

```text
-:26 DEBUG: The canonical weights are: "thin", "extra-light", "light", "normal", "medium", "semi-bold", "bold", "extra-bold", "black"
-:29 DEBUG: There are aliases for the following values: 200, 400, 600, 800, 900
-:32 DEBUG: There are 9 canonical weights
-:35 DEBUG: Is 'demi-bold' a canonical weight? false
-:36 DEBUG: Is there an alias 'demi-bold'? true
-:39 DEBUG: The typographic term 'semi-bold' maps to font-weight: 600
-:48 DEBUG: All weights & aliases: ("thin": 100, "extra-light": 200, "light": 300, "normal": 400, "medium": 500, "semi-bold": 600, "bold": 700, "extra-bold": 800, "black": 900, "regular": 400, "ultra-light": 200, "demi-bold": 600, "ultra-bold": 800, "heavy": 900)
```

I want to draw your attention to two things:

1. To full use maps we also need lists, so it's quite normal to see both modules used together. This makes sense when you consider that the keys for a map are a list.
2. Like lists, **maps are immutable**, so the functions that alter them like `list.set()` and `list.merge()` return entirely new maps that need to be explicitly assigned to a variable if they need to be used later.

## Conditionals with the `@if` & `@else` SASS At-Rules

SASS allows certain styles or be applied conditionally by wrapping the relevant code in `@if` and optionally also `@else` blocks. The syntax is simply:

```scss
@if some-condition {
  // will happend when the condition is truthy
} @else {
  // will happen when the condition is not truthy
}
```

The condition can be anything that produces a value. If that value is a Boolean then it's used directly, if not, then SASS passes the value through it's *truthiness* logic.

### *'Truthiness'* in SASS

SASS is extremely restrictive on what it considers *falsey* — there are exactly two values that evaluate to `false` in SASS — `false` and `null`, literally everything else, including empty strings, empty lists, empty maps, and even `0` evaluate to `true`.

Yes, in SASS, **`0` and empty strings are `true`**! Really!

### Boolean & Comparison Operators

Like with the arithmetic operators, SASS provides the basics, but nothing more (though without major omissions in this case).

There are the usual Boolean operators of `and`, `or`, and `not`.

And then there are the comparison operators:

* `==` for equality, and `!=` for inequality, and the same operators work for all data types
* The usual four comparison operators can be used to compare numbers — `<`, `>`, `<=` & `>=`

And that's all there is!

## Loops with the `@each` & `@for` SASS At-Rules

SASS provides two distinct looping constructs for achieving two distinct ends:

1. `@each` loops over a list or map
2. `@for` repeats an action a specific amount of times

> SASS does also provide an `@while` at-rule, but [the documentation](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/at-rules/control/while/) literally says to avoid using it unless absolutely necessary!
{: .aside}

### Looping Over Lists with `@each`

If you have a list, you can execute some code once for each item in the the list using the following syntax:

```scss
@each $item-name in $list {
  // This happens once for each item in the list
  // The current item will be avaiable via $item-name
}
```

Note that we get to choose the name the item will have each time through the loop, `$item-name` is a placeholder, it could be anything, even `$waffle` 😉

As an example, save the following as `temp.scss`:

```scss
$deserts: waffle, pancake, cake;

@each $desert in $deserts {
  @debug $desert;
}
```

The compile it with `npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss > /dev/null`. The output will be:

```text
-:4 DEBUG: waffle
-:4 DEBUG: pancake
-:4 DEBUG: cake
```

### Looping Over Maps with `@each`

If you have a map you use the same at-rule as for lusts, but you provide two names rather than one — a name for each key, and a name for each matching value:

```scss
@each $key-name, $value-name in $map {
  // this happens one for each key-value pair in the map
  // the current key will be available via $key-name, and the matching current value via $value-name
}
```

Again, note that both `$key-name` and `$value-name` are placeholders, you can use any name that makes sense in the specific situation.

As an example, save the following as `temp.scss`:

```scss
$weight-names: ( // the official names
  "thin": 100,
  "extra-light": 200,
  "light": 300,
  "normal": 400,
  "medium": 500,
  "semi-bold": 600,
  "bold": 700,
  "extra-bold": 800,
  "black": 900
);

@each $weight-name, $weight in $weight-names {
  @debug "The weight '#{$weight-name}' corresponds to font-weight: #{$weight}";
}
```

Again, compile this with `npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss > /dev/null` and you should see the following output:

```text
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'thin' corresponds to font-weight: 100
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'extra-light' corresponds to font-weight: 200
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'light' corresponds to font-weight: 300
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'normal' corresponds to font-weight: 400
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'medium' corresponds to font-weight: 500
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'semi-bold' corresponds to font-weight: 600
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'bold' corresponds to font-weight: 700
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'extra-bold' corresponds to font-weight: 800
-:14 DEBUG: The weight 'black' corresponds to font-weight: 900
```

### Looping over Ranges with `@for`

In SASS, `@for` loops are effectively a short-cut for the most common types of counting loops — those that go from an initial value to a final value in increments of one. You tell `@for` where to start and where to end, and it will move between those two numbers by adding or subtracting `1` each time, depending on whether the first number is larger than the second or not.

The other thing you have to tell `@for` is whether to loop inclusively or exclusively of the final number. If you numbers are `1` and `5` do you want to repeat the code for `1`, `2`, `3`, `4` & `5`, or just for `1`, `2`, `3` & `4`? You express your intention by choosing one of two possibly key-words  — `through` to include the last number, and `to` to exclude it. That gives two possibly syntaxes:

```scss
// inclusive loops
@for $integer-name from $start-integer through $end-integer {
  // this happens once for each integer from the first up-to-and-including the last
  // each time the current number will be available as $number-name
}

// exclusive loops
@for $integer-name from $start-integer to $end-integer {
  // this happens once for each integer from the first up-to-but-not-including the last
  // each time the current number will be available as $number-name
}
```

There are three things to note here:

1. Like with `@each`, the name `$integer-name` is a placeholder, it can be anything, though by convention it's usually simply `$i`.
2. The numbers **must be integers**
3. **The step size is always one**

As an example, save the following as `temp.scss`:

```scss
@debug "Inclusive Loop from 1 through 5:";
@for $i from 1 through 5 {
  @debug " - #{$i}";
}

@debug "Exclusive Loop from 1 to 5:";
@for $i from 1 to 5 {
  @debug " - #{$i}";
}
```

Again, compile with `npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss > /dev/null` and you should see the output:

```text
-:1 DEBUG: Inclusive Loop from 1 through 5:
-:3 DEBUG:  - 1
-:3 DEBUG:  - 2
-:3 DEBUG:  - 3
-:3 DEBUG:  - 4
-:3 DEBUG:  - 5
-:6 DEBUG: Exclusive Loop from 1 to 5:
-:8 DEBUG:  - 1
-:8 DEBUG:  - 2
-:8 DEBUG:  - 3
-:8 DEBUG:  - 4
```

## SASS Templates with `@mixin` & `@include`

When you need to create multiple similar style declarations it can be very convenient to use some kind of template, and that's the functionality the SASS documentation refers to as *Mixins*. You define what is in effect a style template with named arguments with the `@mixin` SASS at-rule, and you use it to generate a style declaration with the `@include` SASS at-rule.

SASS Mixins are often used in conjunction with loops to create suites of related CSS classes. This is how bootstrap creates it's many suites of similar related classes,  like for example the  `.border-1` … `.border-5` classes.

To define a mixin use the following syntax:

```scss
@mixin mixin-name($arg1-name: arg1DefaultValue) {
  // styles declarations here, making use of the argument names as desired
}
```

A few things to note:

1. The list of arguments is entirely optional, but it's rare not to need any at all. Should you just want to have some identical styles you can add anywhere you can completely omit the `()` from the end of the `mixin-name`.
2. The default values for arguments are optional, but **arguments without default values are required**!
3. To make an argument truly optional, so that it doesn't even have a default value, explicitly assign in the default value `null`.

Once a mixin exists, it can be included in any style declaration by using the `@include` SASS at-rule. The syntax is:

```scss
// Include a mixin without arguments
@include mixin-name;

// Include a mixin with arguments
@include mixin-name($arg1-value, $arg2-value);
```

To see This in action, save the following as `temp.scss`:

```scss
@use 'sass:map';

// a mixin with no arguments that defines the standard spacing for blocks
@mixin standard-block-spacing {
  margin: 1rem;
  padding: 1rem;
}

// style regualur paragraphs
p {
  @include standard-block-spacing;
  text-align: justify;
}

// block qotes
p.blockquote {
  @include standard-block-spacing;
  font-size: 1.25rem;
}

// a mixin that supports four arguments:
// - $font-size      : the size to make the heading text (REQUIRED)
// - $font-weight    : the weight to use for the heading (REQUIRED)
// - $colour         : an optional colour that defaults to black
// - $text-transform : a completely optional text transformatino to apply
@mixin heading-text($font-size, $font-weight, $color: black, $text-transform: null){
  font-size: $font-size;
  font-weight: $font-weight;
  color: $color;
  // if a text transform was not passed it will be null, which is falsey
  @if $text-transform {
    text-transform: $text-transform;
  }
}

// define a map which maps heading tags to nested maps of header properties
$standard-headings: (
  "h1": (
    "font-size": 2rem,
    "font-weight": 800,
  ),
  "h2": (
    "font-size": 1.75rem,
    "font-weight": 600,
  ),
  "h3": (
    "font-size": 1.5rem,
    "font-weight": 500,
  ),
  "h4": (
    "font-size": 1.25rem,
    "font-weight": 400,
  )
);

// Loop over the supported headers to geneate the needed styles with the help of the mixin
@each $heading-tag, $heading-details in $standard-headings {
  #{$heading-tag} {
    // apply the heading-text mixin
    @include heading-text(map.get($heading-details, 'font-size'), map.get($heading-details, 'font-weight'), DarkBlue);
  }
}

// create a special heading type for banner headlines, also with the help of the mixin
h1.banner {
  @include heading-text(5em, 200, Blue, capitalize);
}
```

To see the set of styles we've produced, let's compile this SASS straight to standard out so we can see it directly with the command:

```sh
npx sass --stdin --no-source-map < temp.scss
```

This shows our code generated the following CSS styles:

```css
p {
  margin: 1rem;
  padding: 1rem;
  text-align: justify;
}

p.blockquote {
  margin: 1rem;
  padding: 1rem;
  font-size: 1.25rem;
}

h1 {
  font-size: 2rem;
  font-weight: 800;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h2 {
  font-size: 1.75rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h3 {
  font-size: 1.5rem;
  font-weight: 500;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h4 {
  font-size: 1.25rem;
  font-weight: 400;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h1.banner {
  font-size: 5em;
  font-weight: 200;
  color: Blue;
  text-transform: capitalize;
}
```

Notice that the two style declarations defined in our example no-argument mixin `standard-block-spacing` have been inserted into the style declarations for both `<p>` and `<blockquote>` tags.

Notice that our loop over the nested map with heading details generated style declarations for each of our four defined heading tags, and that each one has the three style declarations from the `heading-text` mixin for the two required arguments and the one with a default value, but no value for the fourth completely optional argument. Also, notice our use of interpolation on the left-side of the `:` produced the desired effect, defining styles for `h1` to `h4` from the keys in the map:

```css
h1 {
  font-size: 2rem;
  font-weight: 800;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h2 {
  font-size: 1.75rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h3 {
  font-size: 1.5rem;
  font-weight: 500;
  color: DarkBlue;
}

h4 {
  font-size: 1.25rem;
  font-weight: 400;
  color: DarkBlue;
}
```

Finally, notice that our style definition for banner headings which passes the fourth completely optional text transformation argument to the `header-text` mixin has an extra style definition because the `@if` evaluated to `true`:

```css
h1.banner {
  font-size: 5em;
  font-weight: 200;
  color: Blue;
  text-transform: capitalize;
}
```

## EPISODE BREAK HERE

## Our Playground — TO DO transform into the worked example

In order to be able to experiment with SASS we've going to use SASS to generate the CSS to style a simple sample HTML page.

You'll find the page and an initial SCSS file in the folder `pbs184-playground` in the instalment ZIP.

This folder contains the following files and folders:

1. `index.html` — a sample web page that reads it's styles from `./index.css` (which does not exist yet).
2. `index.scss` — a placeholder SASS file where we will be performing our experiments. We will use `sass` to compile this file to `index.css` with the command: `sass index.scss index.css`
3. `contrib` — a folder containing a copy of utility code from other sources, in this case, just the latest version of [FontAwesome](https://fontawesome.com/) (7.2).

### The Demo HTML

The HTML source code for the demo area has been designed to be as simple as possible while still being complex enough to meaningfully demonstrate how SASS works.

If you open `index.html` in your favourite code editor you'll see how it works.

In the `<head>` section it simply defines the usual metadata, imports Font Awesome, and then tries to load the generated CSS from `index.css`:

```html
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8" />
  <title>PBS 183 SASS Playground</title>

  <!-- Import Font Awesome 7 -->
  <link href="/contrib/fontawesome/css/all.min.css" rel="stylesheet">

  <!-- Import the compiled CSS -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="index.css">
</head>
```

The body uses the semantic HTML tags to break the page's content into two top-level chunks — a `<header>` tag containing the page title and the instructions for compiling the SASS code wrapped in a `<section>` tag, and a `<main>` tag containing two pieces of content each wrapped in `<article>` tags.

Both the `<section>` and `<article>` tags have meaningful IDs so they can be targeted for styling.

For now, the content of each section doesn't matter, so here is the big-picture structure of the page:

```html
<body>
  <header>
    <h1>PBS 183 SASS Playground</h1>

    <section id="instructions">
      <!-- … -->
    </section>
  </header>

  <main>
    <article id="wisdom">
      <!-- … -->
    </article>
    <article id="humor">
      <!-- … -->
    </article>

  </main>
</body>
```

### Getting Started

Before opening the HTML file in a browser to view the resulting styles, the SASS file needs to be compiled to CSS.

We'll be using the NodeJS version of the `sass` command to do that, and as we saw above, we can simply call with with two arguments to do an on-demand conversation — the first argument should be the SASS file, and the second the desired output file.

Let's try an on-demand conversion to verify that SASS is properly installed. 

Start by opening a terminal in the `pbs184-playground` folder, then simply run the command:

```sh
npx sass index.scss index.css
```

If everything is working as expected that should show no output, but create a file named `index.css`.

 When developing it can be quite frustrating to have to keep manually re-compiling your SASS, so I prefer to use `sass`'s watch mode to detect changes to the SASS file and then automatically re-build the CSS. The syntax for *watch mode* is a little different:

```sh
npx sass --watch source_files:output_files
```

Let's set that running now so we can start experimenting:

```sh
npx sass --watch index.scss:index.css
```

SASS should report building two files:

```text
$ npx sass --watch index.scss:index.css
>>> Sass is watching for changes. Press Ctrl-C to stop.
      write index.css
      write index.css.map
```

Notice that the command does not exist after generating the files. It will keep running until you intentionally stop it `crtl+c`.

Also, why did it build two files? We expected it to build  `index.css`, but what's `index.css.map`? It's a so-called source-map file. These kinds of files are used by some developer tools to re-map line numbers in generated files back to the matching lines in the original source code. In this case the file allows IDEs that support source maps to connect lines numbers in `index.css` back to their matching line numbers in `index.scss`.

### Our First SASS → CSS Conversion

Let's take a look at what SASS actually did.

Here's our initial SASS file (`index.scss`):

```scss
/* 
 * This style sheet has been generated from the SASS file ./index.scss.
 * 
 * To re-build this file from the latest version of the SASS file, run one of:
 * - A one-time build   : `npx sass index.scss index.css`
 * - A watch mode build : `npx sass --watch index.scss:index.css`
 */

 //
 // === Page Setup — DO NOT EDIT THIS SECTION ===
 //

/*
 * Import Desired Google Fonts:
 * - Ubuntu Sans      : the sans-serif body font from the PBS site
 * - Ubuntu Mono      : the mono-spaced heading font from the PBS  site
 * - Ubuntu Sans Mono : a mono-spaced sans-serif font that compliments the PBS site typography
 * - Indie Flower     : a handwriting font that compliments the PBS site typorgraphy
 */
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Indie+Flower&family=Ubuntu+Mono:ital,wght@0,400;0,700;1,400;1,700&family=Ubuntu+Sans+Mono:ital,wght@0,400..700;1,400..700&family=Ubuntu+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..800;1,100..800&display=swap');

/*
 * Basic page setup
 */
body {
  font-family: 'Ubuntu Sans', sans-serif;
  margin: 0px;
  padding: 1rem 3rem;
}
h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6 { /* padding replicates Bootstrap convention */
  font-family: 'Ubuntu Mono', monospace;
  color: #00408d;
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
}
p{ /* padding replicates Bootstrap convention */
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
code, pre {
  font-family: "Ubuntu Sans Mono", monospace;
}

//
// === END PAGE SETUP ===
//

//
// === Playground — ADD YOUR SASS CODE BELOW THIS SASS COMMENT ===
//

```

Notice we have a mix of SASS and CSS comments, and, that all the style declarations are standard CSS, noting we have not seen before.

Let's see what that SASS code compiled to in `index.css`:

```css
/* 
 * This style sheet has been generated from the SASS file ./index.scss.
 * 
 * To re-build this file from the latest version of the SASS file, run one of:
 * - A one-time build   : `sass index.scss index.css`
 * - A watch mode build : `sass --watch index.scss:index.css`
 */
/*
 * Import Desired Google Fonts:
 * - Ubuntu Sans      : the sans-serif body font from the PBS site
 * - Ubuntu Mono      : the mono-spaced heading font from the PBS  site
 * - Ubuntu Sans Mono : a mono-spaced sans-serif font that compliments the PBS site typography
 * - Indie Flower     : a handwriting font that compliments the PBS site typorgraphy
 */
@import url("https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Indie+Flower&family=Ubuntu+Mono:ital,wght@0,400;0,700;1,400;1,700&family=Ubuntu+Sans+Mono:ital,wght@0,400..700;1,400..700&family=Ubuntu+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..800;1,100..800&display=swap");
/*
 * Basic page setup
 */
body {
  font-family: 'Ubuntu Sans', sans-serif;
  margin: 0px;
  padding: 1rem 3rem; }

h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6 {
  /* padding replicates Bootstrap convention */
  font-family: 'Ubuntu Mono', monospace;
  color: #00408d;
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }

p {
  /* padding replicates Bootstrap convention */
  margin-top: 0px;
  margin-bottom: 1rem; }

code, pre {
  font-family: "Ubuntu Sans Mono", monospace; }

/*# sourceMappingURL=index.css.map */

```

Notice the SASS comments didn't get mapped through, but CSS comments did. All the CSS code was been passed through un-changed from a functional point of view, but did all get re-formatted into SASS's standard CSS style.

Finally, notice the specially formatted comment at the bottom of the file referencing the source map file, this comment allows IDEs to associate the correct source map file with this CSS file.

Now that we have our generated CSS, open `index.html` in your favourite browser to see the initial HTML page with just the basic styles applied.

### Testing Watch Mode

To prove that the watch-mode build is working, let's add a test style to the playground area of the SASS file:

```scss
//
// === Playground — ADD YOUR SASS CODE BELOW THIS SASS COMMENT ===
//

section#instructions {
    color: silver;
    font-size: 0.5rem;
}
```

Save the file, and you should see the running `sass` command detect the file change and re-build the files:

```text
>>> Change detected to: index.scss
      write index.css
      write index.css.map
```

Refresh the page in your browser and you should see the instruction text at the top of the file shrink to an unusably small size.

This is obviously not a helpful change, so remove the code from the playground file and save again.
